nuance: Do the action within a window before it ends — while it's still light, still warm, still available.

examples.en: I'll hang out the laundry while it's still light.
I'll write down the station's name before I forget.

nuance: Each time X happens, Y follows — a recurring pairing.

examples.en: Every time I look at the Hokkaido photos, I want to go back.
Every time I go to the ward office, a different document is required.

nuance: The instant the first action finished, the second happened — often unexpected.

examples.en: The moment the train doors opened, people surged in.
The moment I stepped outside, it started to rain.

connection: V-ている / N-の + 最中に
nuance: Interrupted right at the peak of doing something.

examples.en: The phone rang right in the middle of my job interview.
Right in the middle of cooking, the share house fire alarm went off.

nuance: After the last action, the expected follow-up never came.

examples.en: I haven't been in touch with my coworker since we met last month.
I bought the study book but haven't opened it even once since.

meaning: for the first time in (a period)
nuance: After a long gap, doing the thing again — emphasises how much time passed.

examples.en: I returned to Japan for the first time in three years.
It cleared up for the first time in a week, so I aired out the futon.

meaning: while at it / taking the opportunity
connection: V-dict/V-た / N-の + ついでに
nuance: Do a secondary thing while already out doing the main one.

examples.en: While I'm out shopping, I'll drop off the package at the konbini.
While on my walk, I checked out the nearby park too.

nuance: Sets up a hypothesis to reason from: assuming that were true, then...

examples.en: Supposing I move, a cheaper-rent area would be better.
If I assume I work weekends, does the hourly wage go up?

nuance: たとえ signals the coming ても — 'even supposing X, still Y'.

examples.en: Even if it rains, I'll go to the interview.
Even if the pay is low, I want to keep this job.

nuance: Highlights an extreme or minimal example — 'even this much'.

examples.en: I was so tired I couldn't even write my own name.
I'm so busy I don't even have time to drink water.

nuance: Just this one condition is enough for the result.

examples.en: As long as I have the money, I can live in this share house.
As long as I have time, I'll study every day.

nuance: Two things change together, gradually and in step.

examples.en: As summer approaches, the humidity increases.
As I got used to Japanese, daily life became easier.

nuance: Increasing the first proportionally increases the second.

examples.en: The more I think about it, the less I understand.
The more you practice, the better you get.

nuance: Nothing else reaches that degree — the ultimate example.

examples.en: Nothing is as tiring as a packed train.
There's no season as gloomy as the rainy season.

meaning: to the extent that / so … that
nuance: Gives a vivid measure of how extreme something is.

examples.en: I walked so much that my legs hurt.
I was so happy I could have cried.

nuance: Downplays: nothing in particular, no strong feeling either way.

examples.en: I don't particularly want to go.
This izakaya isn't particularly expensive.

nuance: Adds a second, often bigger, item beyond the first.

examples.en: This konbini sells not only food but also daily goods.
He can speak not only English but also Japanese.

nuance: Since the situation is fixed, a natural obligation follows — 'now that X, of course Y'.

examples.en: Now that I've promised, I'll see it through to the end.
Since I work in Japan, I have to pay taxes.

connection: plain form / N-の + くせに
nuance: Like のに but scornful — implies the subject should know or do better.

examples.en: Even though he's a newbie, he acts so cocky.
Even though he doesn't know the way, he tries to lead.

connection: plain form / N-の + わりに
nuance: The result is unexpected given the standard you'd assume.

examples.en: For how small it is, this room's rent is high.
For how young he is, he's very composed.

connection: plain form / N-の + おかげで
nuance: Credits a good outcome to a cause — grateful tone.

examples.en: Thanks to my coworker, I got used to the work.
Thanks to this study book, I passed the exam.

connection: plain form / N-の + せいで
nuance: Blames a bad outcome on a cause — the opposite of おかげで.

examples.en: Because I overslept, I missed the train.
Because of the typhoon, the flight was cancelled.

connection: plain form + からといって
nuance: Rejects a too-easy conclusion: X being true doesn't automatically give Y.

examples.en: Just because I live in Tokyo doesn't mean I know all the streets.
Just because it's cheap doesn't mean the quality is bad.

nuance: Introduces a term to define or explain it.

examples.en: 'Shikikin' means the security deposit you pay up front.
A 'zairyu card' is a foreigner's ID.

nuance: Wraps a whole clause into a noun — the fact/idea that ...

examples.en: I heard the fact that he's quitting.
The fact that rent is going up is a big deal.

nuance: Reassures: there's no need to do that; don't bother.

examples.en: There's no need to hurry; there's time until the last train.
There's no need to worry about it alone.

nuance: Draws a natural conclusion from a fact — 'so that's the reason'.

examples.en: So the heater's broken. No wonder it's cold.
He's been in Tokyo ten years. That's why he knows the streets.

connection: plain form + わけではない
nuance: Partial denial: it's not the case that ... entirely.

examples.en: It's not that I dislike Japanese food.
It doesn't mean I work overtime every single day.

connection: V-dict + わけにはいかない
nuance: Can't do it for social or moral reasons, even if physically possible.

examples.en: I have an interview tomorrow, so I can't very well skip it.
I can't very well turn down my boss's invitation.

nuance: Confident logical denial — there's no way that's true.

examples.en: Someone as diligent as him can't possibly be late.
There's no way a shop is open at this hour.

connection: plain form / N + に違いない
nuance: Strong conviction based on evidence — surely that's it.

examples.en: The streets are wet. It must have rained overnight.
He must be from Hokkaido, no doubt.

nuance: Exactly as specified — following the model, plan, or instruction.

examples.en: I assembled it just as the manual said.
I transferred at the station just as my senior told me.

nuance: The volitional + とする marks an attempt or the moment right before an action begins — often a try that fails or a state on the verge of happening.

examples.en: Just as I was about to board the last train, the doors closed.
I try to speak in Japanese, but the words just won't come out.

nuance: V-stem + かけ describes something started but left partway through — a half-drunk coffee, an unfinished sentence.

examples.en: I left my half-drunk coffee on the desk and headed out.
He started to say something, then suddenly went quiet.

nuance: がち marks an unwanted tendency — something that happens all too often, usually with a negative flavor.

examples.en: In winter I tend to catch colds.
After a night shift, I tend to skip breakfast.

nuance: 気味 (rendaku ぎ) adds 'a slight sense of' — a mild, noticeable tendency toward the noun or state.

examples.en: Lately I've been a bit worn out from all the overtime.
I feel a cold coming on, so I'll head home early today.

nuance: っぽい means '-like' or 'has a strong quality of' — childish, forgetful, reddish. Often carries a mildly critical tone.

examples.en: This sweater looks a bit childish.
He's forgetful, so I check with him again and again.

nuance: 続ける attaches to a verb stem to mean 'continue doing' — the action goes on without stopping.

examples.en: I keep studying Japanese every morning.
The train wouldn't come, so I kept waiting at the station.

nuance: 出す on a verb stem marks a sudden, often unexpected onset of an action — 'burst out ...ing'.

examples.en: The sky suddenly darkened and it started to rain.
The baby next to me suddenly burst out crying.

nuance: 切る on a verb stem means carrying an action fully through to completion — finish off, use up entirely.

examples.en: I read the whole long novel through in one night.
I managed to run the marathon all the way to the finish.

nuance: 合う on a verb stem expresses a reciprocal or mutual action — doing something to or with each other.

examples.en: I split the housework with the other share-house residents.
When we're in trouble, my friends and I help each other out.

nuance: 直す on a verb stem means doing the action again, usually to correct or improve it — 'redo', 'do over'.

examples.en: I made a mistake, so I rewrote the document.
My pronunciation was off, so I said it over again in front of the teacher.

nuance: The humblest way to say 'I will do' — literally 'receive the favor of being allowed to do', common in service and workplace keigo.

examples.en: Today, I will be the one to show you around (humbly).
Might I be allowed to leave a little early tomorrow?

nuance: The passive れる・られる form doubles as a light honorific — a softer way to elevate someone's action than お〜になる.

examples.en: Has the store manager already gone home? (honorific)
The president reads the newspaper every morning. (honorific)

connection: V-ない stem + ずに (する→せずに)
nuance: A more formal, written equivalent of 〜ないで meaning 'without doing'. Note the irregular する → せずに.

examples.en: I went to work without eating breakfast.
Don't worry about anything and just leave it to me.

nuance: A non-contiguous pattern: なかなか paired with a negative predicate means something just won't happen despite effort or expectation.

examples.en: The bus just won't come.
Kanji are hard, and I just can't seem to memorize them.

nuance: Dictionary form + ところだった describes something that nearly happened but was avoided — 'I almost ...'.

examples.en: I almost missed the last train.
I very nearly dropped my wallet.

nuance: て-form + ばかりいる criticizes someone for doing only one thing all the time and nothing else.

examples.en: My little brother does nothing but play video games at home.
He does nothing but complain and never actually does anything.

nuance: て-form + はじめて marks the point where something first became true or understood — 'only after ... did I ...'.

examples.en: Only after living alone did I understand how much my parents did for me.
It was only after coming to Japan that I first tried natto.

nuance: ように言う reports an indirect request or instruction — 'tell someone to (not) do something'.

examples.en: The doctor told me to cut back on alcohol.
The manager told me not to be late.

connection: V-て + いただけませんか
nuance: A very polite request: て-form + いただけませんか literally asks 'could I not receive the favor of you doing ...?' — the softest 'could you please'.

examples.en: Excuse me, could you kindly take a photo for me?
Could you kindly explain it to me one more time?

nuance: について marks the topic a statement, question, or research is *about* — neutral and common in reports and study.

examples.en: I asked about my visa at the ward office.
I'm studying about Japanese culture.

nuance: に対して directs an attitude or action *toward* a target, or sets one thing *in contrast* against another.

examples.en: The store manager is always polite toward customers.
My brother likes meat. In contrast, I like fish.

nuance: にとって frames a judgment from a person or group’s standpoint — "for X, this is …".

examples.en: For a foreign student, rent is expensive.
For me, Tokyo summers are too hot.

nuance: によって covers three jobs: the agent in a passive ("done by"), variation ("depending on"), and cause ("due to").

examples.en: The price differs depending on the shop.
The trains stopped due to the typhoon.

nuance: によると cites a source of information; the sentence usually ends in a hearsay そうだ / らしい.

examples.en: According to the forecast, it will rain tomorrow.
According to my friend, that izakaya is cheap.

nuance: として states the role, capacity, or category in which someone or something acts.

examples.en: I work as an interpreter.
I'm using this room as a bedroom.

nuance: 代わりに swaps one thing for another, or offsets one act with a return act ("in exchange").

examples.en: I paid with an IC card instead of cash.
I'll clean. In exchange, you cook.

nuance: はもちろん takes one obvious item for granted, then adds a second — "A of course, and even B".

examples.en: This shop is crowded in the daytime, not to mention at night.
He can speak Japanese, let alone English.

nuance: なんか cites an example casually, often with a dismissive or self-deprecating tone ("stuff like…", "someone like me").

examples.en: On my day off I don't want to do stuff like cleaning.
Someone like me still can't read kanji.

nuance: こそ singles out its word for emphasis — "this one, precisely" — often to correct or to reciprocate ("こちらこそ").

examples.en: This year, precisely, I want to go to Hokkaido.

ja: ありがとうございます
examples.en: The thanks are mine — thank *you*.

nuance: だって is a casual even/too — "even N" or "N too". Lists split it between N3 and N2, so treat the level as unsettled.

examples.en: Even a child can read that kanji.
I want to go to the izakaya too, you know.

meaning: casual quotative / topic "you said / speaking of"
nuance: って is casual と/という — it quotes ("X said …"), names ("a thing called X"), or raises a topic ("speaking of X").

examples.en: The manager said tomorrow is a day off.
Share houses — are they cheap?

nuance: 向け marks the intended audience a thing was *made for* ("designed for beginners"), unlike 向き which is mere suitability.

examples.en: This is a map made for foreigners.
I'm looking for a Japanese class aimed at beginners.

nuance: 向き describes natural suitability ("suited for beginners", "facing south") — a fit, not a deliberate target like 向け.

examples.en: This room faces south and is bright.
This job is suited for students.

nuance: 的 turns a noun into a な-adjective ("economic", "personal") — な before nouns, に as an adverb.

examples.en: Personally, I like this shop.
I'm moving for economic reasons.

nuance: さ turns an adjective into a noun measuring its degree — 高い → 高さ (height), 便利 → 便利さ (convenience).

examples.en: I was surprised by the heat of Tokyo summers.
I've gotten used to the convenience of the trains.

connection: N + 中 (家中, 一日中)
nuance: 中 read じゅう means "all through" a whole span or space (一日中 = all day, 家中 = the whole house) — distinct from ちゅう "in the middle of".

examples.en: I studied in my room all day.
The cat ran all over the house.

nuance: ごとに means "at each interval" — 一駅ごとに (every station), 会うごとに (each time we meet).

examples.en: This train stops at every station.
I take out the burnable garbage every three days.

nuance: はずだった states an expectation that failed to happen — "it was supposed to …, but …".

examples.en: I was supposed to meet my friend today, but it was called off because of rain.
The train was supposed to arrive at nine.

connection: V-dict + べき(だ) (する→すべき)
nuance: べき states a strong should/ought — a duty or the right thing to do. With する it becomes すべき (or するべき).

examples.en: You should go to the ward office before moving.
You ought to start saving money now.

nuance: に比べて sets a baseline for comparison — "compared to X, Y is …".

examples.en: Compared to last year, rent has risen.
Compared to Tokyo, Hokkaido is cool.
